lessons.title: Why Projects Need Light Structure: The Triple Constraint
lessons.content:
Imagine you are throwing a party. You could just hope it comes together, but the good ones usually have a little structure: a guest list, a date, and a budget. A **project** is any piece of work with a goal and an end, like a party, a trip, a renovation, or a group assignment. Getting that work done on time with other people goes far more smoothly with a small amount of planning. Not a mountain of paperwork, just enough to keep everyone pointed the same way.

The core idea is the **triple constraint**. Every project balances three things that pull against each other: **scope** (what you do, how much), **time** (by when), and **cost** (how much money or how many people). Sitting in the middle is **quality** (how good the result is). The key truth: change one, and the others have to move. You cannot add work for free.

Back to the party. If more guests show up (that is more **scope**), you need a bigger budget (more **cost**) or more time to prepare, or the food and the experience suffer (lower **quality**). A renovation is the same: add a new room to the plan and either the deadline slips, the bill grows, or the finish gets rushed. A beginner mistake is to assume you can pile on extra work while keeping the same deadline, the same budget, and the same quality. Something always gives.

So the first skill is simple awareness: when someone wants more, ask what gives. More scope means more time, more cost, or less quality. Naming that trade out loud turns a vague hope into an honest plan.

items.prompt: What is a 'project'?
choices: A piece of work with a goal and an end, like a party, a trip, or a renovation
Any task that can never be finished
A list of people you know
A single quick chore with no planning at all
items.explanation: A project is work aimed at a goal that eventually ends, such as planning a party or redoing a room. It is not an endless task, not just a list of people, and it is bigger than a single quick chore, which is exactly why a little structure helps.

items.prompt: The 'triple constraint' of a project is made up of which three things?
choices: Color, shape, and size
Scope, time, and cost (with quality in the middle)
Speed, noise, and weight
Guests, music, and food only
items.explanation: The triple constraint is scope (what you do), time (by when), and cost (how much), with quality affected in the middle. The other options are not the standard three; quality sits at the center and moves when any of the three changes.

items.prompt: You add more scope to a project but keep the same time and the same cost. What most likely happens?
choices: Everything stays exactly the same, because scope is free
The project automatically finishes sooner
Quality suffers, or the deadline slips, because you cannot add work for free
The cost drops on its own
items.explanation: Adding scope without more time or cost forces something to give, usually quality or the deadline; you cannot add work for free. Scope is never free, more work does not finish sooner, and piling on work does not lower cost.

items.prompt: At a party, inviting many more guests is an increase in which part of the triple constraint?
choices: Time
Cost
Quality
Scope
items.explanation: More guests means more work and more to provide, which is an increase in scope (what you do and how much). It is not the deadline (time) or the money by itself (cost), and quality is the result that suffers if you do not also add time or cost.

items.prompt: In the triple constraint, what does 'quality' refer to, and where does it sit?
choices: How good the result is, sitting in the middle and affected when scope, time, or cost change
The exact day the project must end
The total amount of money available
The number of people you invited
items.explanation: Quality is how good the result turns out, and it sits in the middle, squeezed when scope, time, or cost shift. The end day is time, the money is cost, and the number of people relates to scope, not quality.

items.prompt: A friend says, 'I'll add three more rooms to the renovation, but keep the same deadline, the same budget, and the same finish quality.' What is the flaw?
choices: There is no flaw; you can always add work without any cost
The flaw is caring about the budget at all
The flaw is assuming you can add scope while time, cost, and quality all stay the same, when changing one forces the others to move
The flaw is that renovations are always cheap
items.explanation: Adding scope while holding time, cost, and quality fixed ignores the triple constraint, where changing one forces another to move. You cannot add work for free, caring about the budget is sensible, and renovations are not automatically cheap.

items.prompt: Why does even a small project benefit from a little structure, like a plan or a budget?
choices: Because structure guarantees nothing will ever go wrong
Because it keeps people pointed at the same goal and helps the work get done on time
Because more paperwork always means a better result
Because it removes the need to ever make a choice
items.explanation: Light structure keeps everyone aligned and helps finish on time, like a guest list and date for a party. It does not guarantee nothing goes wrong, piling on paperwork is not the goal, and you still have to make choices.

items.prompt: Someone asks you to add a lot of extra work to a group assignment. What is the most honest response that respects the triple constraint?
choices: Sure, no problem, it will take exactly the same time and effort
That is impossible to even think about
Okay, but what gives? We will need more time, more help, or we accept lower quality
We should secretly skip the original work instead
items.explanation: Honest planning means naming the trade: more scope needs more time, more help (cost), or it costs quality. Claiming it takes the same effort ignores the constraint, calling it impossible to consider is unhelpful, and secretly dropping the original work hides the trade rather than facing it.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation best shows the triple constraint in action?
choices: Choosing what color to paint a wall
Planning a trip where adding more stops means a bigger budget or a longer trip, or a more rushed visit at each place
Deciding the cheapest option is always best
Refusing to plan anything at all
items.explanation: Adding stops (scope) to a trip pushes up cost or time, or makes each visit rushed (quality), which is the triple constraint exactly. Picking a paint color is not about the trade, 'cheapest is always best' is a different idea, and refusing to plan is not an example of balancing the three.

lessons.title: Break It Down and Estimate: Tasks, Dependencies, and the Planning Fallacy
lessons.content:
A huge goal like 'redo the kitchen' is overwhelming because you cannot see how to start or how far along you are. The fix is to **break it down**: split the big goal into smaller **tasks**, the bite-sized pieces of work you can estimate, hand to someone, and check off. Small tasks make progress visible. A renovation becomes demo, then wiring, then drywall, then paint. Now you can see the plan.

Some tasks have **dependencies**, meaning one must wait for another to finish first. You cannot paint before the wall is up, and you cannot put up the wall before the wiring is done. Spotting these in plain words keeps you from getting stuck. A **milestone** is a meaningful checkpoint along the way, like 'walls finished' or 'invitations sent', that tells you a real chunk is done.

Now, **estimation**: guessing how long each task will take. This is genuinely hard, and people are wrong in a predictable direction. We are almost always too optimistic. This is the **planning fallacy**: 'this will take ten minutes' that quietly becomes an hour. A few plain tips help. Break work into small pieces, because small tasks are easier to estimate than one giant blob. Use past experience, since last time is a good clue. Add a **buffer**, extra time for the surprises you cannot predict. And treat an estimate as a **range**, not a promise carved in stone.

One more famous warning: adding people to a project that is already late can actually make it later, because new people need to be brought up to speed and coordinated. More hands is not always faster. The takeaway: break the goal into estimable tasks, respect what must come first, and plan with a buffer.

items.prompt: Why is breaking a big goal into smaller tasks helpful?
choices: Because a huge vague goal is hard to manage, while small tasks can be estimated, assigned, and checked off so progress is visible
Because it makes the project take much longer on purpose
Because small tasks never have to be done
Because it hides how much work is left
items.explanation: Splitting a goal into tasks turns something overwhelming into pieces you can estimate, assign, and track, making progress visible. It is not meant to waste time, the tasks still must be done, and breaking down reveals rather than hides the remaining work.

items.prompt: What is a 'dependency' between two tasks?
choices: Two tasks that are exactly the same
A task that can be skipped entirely
When one task must wait for another to finish first, like painting waiting for the wall to be up
The total cost of the whole project
items.explanation: A dependency means one task cannot start until another is done, such as paint waiting on the wall. It is not two identical tasks, not a skippable task, and not the project's cost; it is about ordering.

items.prompt: In a renovation, which ordering respects the dependencies?
choices: Paint first, then build the wall, then do the wiring
Do the wiring and build the wall before painting it
Paint and demo at the very same instant with no order
Skip the wall and paint the empty air
items.explanation: The wall must exist (and the wiring be done) before you can paint it, so wiring and wall come before paint. Painting before the wall exists, ignoring order entirely, or painting with no wall all break the dependencies.

items.prompt: What is a 'milestone' in a project plan?
choices: A meaningful checkpoint that shows a real chunk of work is done, like 'walls finished'
The smallest possible piece of work
A task that has been cancelled
The money left in the budget
items.explanation: A milestone is a meaningful checkpoint marking that a significant chunk is complete, such as 'invitations sent'. It is not the smallest task, not a cancelled task, and not the remaining budget.

items.prompt: What is the 'planning fallacy'?
choices: The belief that planning is never useful
The tendency to estimate too optimistically, so tasks take longer than we guess
A rule that every estimate is always exactly right
The idea that tasks always finish early
items.explanation: The planning fallacy is our systematic optimism: we guess too low, so 'ten minutes' becomes an hour. It does not say planning is useless, estimates are not always right, and tasks tend to run long, not finish early.

items.prompt: Because estimates are uncertain, you should treat an estimate as:
choices: A firm promise that must never be wrong
A reason to never start the work
A range that you back up with a buffer, not a promise carved in stone
A number that is always far too high
items.explanation: Estimates are best treated as ranges with a buffer added for surprises, not as guarantees. Treating an estimate as an unbreakable promise ignores its uncertainty, it is no reason to avoid the work, and estimates usually run low, not too high.

items.prompt: Which is a good habit for making better estimates?
choices: Estimate one giant blob of work instead of small pieces
Break work into small tasks, use past experience, and add a buffer for the unknown
Always assume nothing will go wrong
Refuse to estimate and just hope
items.explanation: Small tasks estimate better, past experience guides you, and a buffer covers surprises. Estimating one giant blob is harder and less accurate, assuming nothing goes wrong is the optimism trap, and refusing to estimate leaves you with no plan.

items.prompt: A group assignment is running late. Someone suggests adding three new people to the team to finish faster. Why might this backfire?
choices: Because more people always finishes a late project faster, with no downside
Because new people need to be brought up to speed and coordinated, which can actually make a late project later
Because new people can never do any useful work
Because the deadline will automatically move for you
items.explanation: Adding people to a late project adds time for onboarding and coordination, which can make it later, not faster. More people is not always faster, new people can contribute eventually, and deadlines do not move on their own.

items.prompt: You guess a task will take ten minutes, but tasks like it usually take an hour. What is the smartest move?
choices: Insist on ten minutes because that was your first guess
Refuse to do the task at all
Use past experience and a buffer, and plan for closer to an hour as a range
Add five more people to make ten minutes happen
items.explanation: Past experience says these tasks run about an hour, so a buffered, range-based estimate near an hour is realistic. Clinging to the optimistic first guess is the planning fallacy, refusing the task does not help, and piling on people does not shrink the time and can even add coordination delay.

lessons.title: Plan-All vs Short Cycles: Agile, Kanban, and Adapting
lessons.content:
Once work is broken into tasks, how do you actually run it? Two broad styles, plus the habit that keeps either one honest.

The old style is **waterfall**: plan everything up front, then build it in order, step by step, all the way to the end. This is fine when the requirements are fixed and well known, like a checklist that will not change. The risk is that you discover problems late, near the end, after a lot is already built. Imagine cooking a whole feast blind, no tasting, and only finding out at dinner that it is too salty.

**Agile** is the other style: work in short cycles, often called **iterations** or **sprints**, and at the end of each cycle deliver a small working piece, get **feedback**, and adjust. You taste as you cook and fix the seasoning along the way. Agile embraces change, so it shines when the requirements might shift. The plain contrast is plan-everything-first versus short-cycles-with-feedback.

To see and control the work, many teams use a **kanban board**: a simple board with columns, usually To Do, then Doing, then Done. Each task is a card, and you move it across as it progresses. The key rule is to **limit work-in-progress (WIP)**, meaning cap how many things are in the Doing column at once. Too much multitasking slows everything down, so the principle is: see the work, and finish things before starting new ones. Think sticky notes on a wall, or a chores board.

Finally, **track and adapt**. A plan is a guess you update, not a promise carved in stone. Check progress regularly, surface **blockers** (things stopping a task) early, and re-plan when reality differs from the plan. A quick daily check-in, 'what's done, what's next, what's blocked', keeps a team aligned, like checking a trip itinerary against reality and rerouting. In short: balance scope, time, and cost; break work into estimable tasks; prefer short feedback cycles when things can change; visualize and limit work-in-progress; and keep tracking and adapting as you learn.

items.prompt: What does the 'waterfall' way of running a project mean?
choices: Plan everything up front, then build it in order to the end
Do no planning and just start building randomly
Deliver a small piece every few days and adjust
Always add more people whenever you fall behind
items.explanation: Waterfall plans everything first and then builds in sequence, which suits fixed, well-known requirements. It is not 'no planning', it is not the short-cycle approach (that is agile), and it is not about adding people.

items.prompt: The requirements for a project are likely to change as you learn more. Which approach fits best, and why?
choices: Waterfall, because it is always the best choice no matter what
Neither; changing requirements mean you should not plan at all
Agile, because short cycles deliver a piece, get feedback, and adjust, so it handles change well
Waterfall, because agile means doing no planning
items.explanation: Agile's short cycles and feedback let you adjust as requirements shift, which is exactly when it shines. Waterfall is not always best (it struggles with change), changing requirements still need planning, and agile is short-cycle planning, not the absence of planning.

items.prompt: In agile, what is an 'iteration' (or 'sprint')?
choices: The single long phase at the very end of a project
A short cycle in which you deliver a small working piece, get feedback, and adjust
A meeting where no work happens
A promise that the plan will never change
items.explanation: An iteration or sprint is a short cycle that produces a small working piece, gathers feedback, and leads to adjustments. It is not one long end phase, not a meeting by itself, and not a promise to freeze the plan; agile expects change.

items.prompt: Why is waterfall risky when requirements can change?
choices: Because it delivers a working piece too often
Because it never involves any building
Because you plan and build everything before checking, so problems are discovered late, after a lot is already built
Because it forces you to taste and adjust constantly
items.explanation: Waterfall commits to a full plan and builds it before real feedback, so changes and problems surface late, like a feast you only taste at dinner. It does not deliver frequent pieces, it does involve building, and it lacks the constant tasting that agile provides.

items.prompt: What does a kanban board do?
choices: It hides the work so the team is not distracted
It visualizes the work as cards moving across columns like To Do, Doing, and Done
It automatically finishes tasks for you
It replaces the need to ever talk to your team
items.explanation: A kanban board makes work visible by showing task cards moving from To Do to Doing to Done. It reveals rather than hides the work, it does not complete tasks by itself, and it supports communication rather than replacing it.

items.prompt: Why do kanban boards limit work-in-progress (cap how many tasks are in 'Doing' at once)?
choices: Because doing many things at once is always faster
Because there is no real reason; it is just decoration
Because too much multitasking slows everything down, so you finish things before starting new ones
Because tasks should never reach the Done column
items.explanation: Limiting work-in-progress fights the slowdown from too much multitasking, nudging the team to finish work before starting more. More multitasking is not faster, the limit is meaningful not decorative, and the goal is to move cards to Done, not avoid it.

items.prompt: A teammate is juggling six tasks at once and finishing almost none. What does the kanban principle suggest?
choices: Start three more tasks, since multitasking is faster
Limit work-in-progress and finish some tasks before starting new ones
Delete the Done column so nothing looks unfinished
Hide the board so no one notices the pile-up
items.explanation: The kanban fix is to cap work-in-progress and finish what is started before pulling in more, which gets cards to Done. Starting more assumes multitasking is faster (it is not), deleting Done hides progress, and hiding the board defeats the point of visualizing work.

items.prompt: What is the healthiest way to think about a project plan as the work goes on?
choices: A promise carved in stone that must never change
A guess you update as reality comes in, re-planning when things differ
Something to write once and then never look at again
A reason to ignore any problems that come up
items.explanation: A plan is best treated as a guess you keep updating, re-planning when reality differs from it. It is not an unchangeable promise, not a write-once document to forget, and not an excuse to ignore problems; surfacing them early is the point.

items.prompt: What is the point of a quick daily check-in like 'what's done, what's next, what's blocked'?
choices: To fill time with a long meeting
To assign blame for anything that went wrong
To keep the team aligned and surface blockers early so you can re-plan
To prove the plan was perfect and needs no changes
items.explanation: A short check-in keeps everyone aligned and brings blockers to light early, so the team can adapt, like checking an itinerary and rerouting. It is meant to be quick not long, it is not about blame, and it assumes the plan may need updating rather than being perfect.

items.prompt: Which short phrase best captures the plain contrast between waterfall and agile?
choices: Cheap-versus-expensive
Plan-everything-first versus short-cycles-with-feedback
More-people-versus-fewer-people
Big-board-versus-small-board
items.explanation: Waterfall plans everything up front while agile works in short cycles with feedback, so 'plan-everything-first versus short-cycles-with-feedback' nails the contrast. The other phrases describe cost, team size, or board size, which are not the core difference between the two approaches.
